title: Auto complete system
description: Suggest words based on prefix using Trie

examples.explanation: 1. Initialize results = []. 2. Check 'cat': len('cat')=3 >= len('cat')=3, 'cat'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add 'cat' → results=['cat']. 3. Check 'catalog': len=7 >=3, 'catalog'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add → results=['cat','catalog']. 4. Check 'category': len=8 >=3, 'category'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add → results=['cat','catalog','category']. 5. Check 'dog': len=3 >=3 but 'dog'[:3]='dog' ≠ 'cat' → skip. 6. Check 'caterpillar': len=11 >=3, 'caterpillar'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add → results=['cat','catalog','category','caterpillar']. 7. Return results.

1. Initialize results = []. 2. Empty prefix case: len('')=0. 3. Check 'apple': len('apple')=5 >=0, 'apple'[:0]='' equals '' → add 'apple' → results=['apple']. 4. Check 'banana': len=6 >=0, 'banana'[:0]='' equals '' → add → results=['apple','banana']. 5. Check 'cherry': len=6 >=0, 'cherry'[:0]='' equals '' → add → results=['apple','banana','cherry']. 6. Return results.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all words in the dictionary and check if each word starts with the given prefix. Use it for small datasets or as a baseline for understanding the problem.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n*m) where n is number of words and m is average word length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Autocomplete System
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of prefix-based word suggestions using brute force string comparison
ladders.explanation: This is the complete autocomplete system using the brute force O(n*m) approach. The intuition is straightforward: when given a prefix, we need to find all words in our dictionary that start with that exact sequence of characters. Since we're using brute force, we simply iterate through every word in the dictionary and check if it begins with the given prefix. This works because the prefix matching condition is binary - either a word starts with the prefix or it doesn't. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty results list. 2) For each word in the dictionary: a) Check if the word length is at least as long as the prefix. b) Compare the first len(prefix) characters of the word with the prefix. c) If they match exactly, add the word to results. 3) Return the results list. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is number of words and m is average word length, since in worst case we examine every character of every word. Space complexity is O(1) excluding input storage, as we only store the results. This connects to the real-world scenario where you might use this for small datasets or as a baseline before optimizing with tries. Common mistakes include: not handling empty prefix (should return all words), not considering case sensitivity (usually we want case-insensitive matching), and inefficient string slicing. The real code pattern: def autocomplete(prefix, dictionary): results = []; for word in dictionary: if len(word) >= len(prefix) and word[:len(prefix)] == prefix: results.append(word); return results

examples.trace: 1. Initialize results = []. 2. Check 'cat': len('cat')=3 >= len('cat')=3, 'cat'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add 'cat' → results=['cat']. 3. Check 'catalog': len=7 >=3, 'catalog'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add → results=['cat','catalog']. 4. Check 'category': len=8 >=3, 'category'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add → results=['cat','catalog','category']. 5. Check 'dog': len=3 >=3 but 'dog'[:3]='dog' ≠ 'cat' → skip. 6. Check 'caterpillar': len=11 >=3, 'caterpillar'[:3]='cat' equals 'cat' → add → results=['cat','catalog','category','caterpillar']. 7. Return results.

1. Initialize results = []. 2. Empty prefix case: len('')=0. 3. Check 'apple': len('apple')=5 >=0, 'apple'[:0]='' equals '' → add 'apple' → results=['apple']. 4. Check 'banana': len=6 >=0, 'banana'[:0]='' equals '' → add → results=['apple','banana']. 5. Check 'cherry': len=6 >=0, 'cherry'[:0]='' equals '' → add → results=['apple','banana','cherry']. 6. Return results.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prefix Matching Function
ladders.desc: Function that checks if one string starts with another string
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function: checking if a word starts with a given prefix. The intuition is that we need to compare the beginning of the word character-by-character with the prefix. This works because strings are sequences, and prefix matching means the first k characters of the word must exactly match the k characters of the prefix. The algorithm: 1) If word length < prefix length, return False immediately (word can't contain the prefix). 2) For i from 0 to len(prefix)-1: compare word[i] with prefix[i]. 3) If any characters differ, return False. 4) If all characters match, return True. Time complexity is O(m) where m is prefix length (worst case we check every character). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being the core comparison used for each dictionary word. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not checking length first (which causes index errors), and case sensitivity issues. Real code pattern: def starts_with(word, prefix): if len(word) < len(prefix): return False; for i in range(len(prefix)): if word[i] != prefix[i]: return False; return True

examples.trace: 1. len('catalog')=7, len('cat')=3 → 7>=3, continue. 2. i=0: word[0]='c', prefix[0]='c' → match. 3. i=1: word[1]='a', prefix[1]='a' → match. 4. i=2: word[2]='t', prefix[2]='t' → match. 5. All characters matched → return True.
1. len('dog')=3, len('cat')=3 → 3>=3, continue. 2. i=0: word[0]='d', prefix[0]='c' → mismatch → return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Linear Search Through Dictionary
ladders.desc: Iterating through all words and collecting matches
ladders.explanation: The central insight of the brute force approach: we need to examine every word in the dictionary to find all matches. The intuition is that without any indexing or preprocessing, the only way to guarantee we find all words starting with a prefix is to check every single word. This works because the search space is the entire dictionary, and each word either matches or doesn't. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty results list. 2) For each word in dictionary: call starts_with(word, prefix). 3) If True, append word to results. 4) After loop, return results. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is dictionary size and m is average prefix length (since starts_with is O(m)). Space complexity is O(k) for results where k is number of matches. This is the core of L0 - it's the main loop that applies the prefix check to every word. Common mistakes: modifying the dictionary while iterating, not handling empty results properly, and inefficient appending. Real code pattern: results = []; for word in dictionary: if starts_with(word, prefix): results.append(word); return results

examples.trace: 1. results=[]. 2. Check 'apple': starts_with('apple','ap')=True → append → results=['apple']. 3. Check 'banana': starts_with('banana','ap')=False → skip. 4. Check 'apricot': starts_with('apricot','ap')=True → append → results=['apple','apricot']. 5. Check 'application': starts_with('application','ap')=True → append → results=['apple','apricot','application']. 6. Return results.

1. results=[]. 2. Check 'apple': starts_with('apple','z')=False → skip. 3. Check 'banana': starts_with('banana','z')=False → skip. 4. Check 'cherry': starts_with('cherry','z')=False → skip. 5. Return empty results [].

ladders.title: Building Block — String Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Comparing two strings character by character
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is comparing two strings to check if they're equal up to a certain length. The intuition is that strings are arrays of characters, so we can iterate through indices and compare corresponding characters. This works because string equality is defined as all characters being identical in the same positions. The algorithm for comparing first k characters: 1) If either string is shorter than k, return False. 2) For i from 0 to k-1: if str1[i] != str2[i], return False. 3) If loop completes, return True. Time complexity is O(k). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2's starts_with function, which uses this comparison for the prefix length. Common mistakes: not validating string lengths first, using wrong comparison operators, and off-by-one errors in loop range. Real code pattern: def compare_prefix(str1, str2, k): if len(str1) < k or len(str2) < k: return False; for i in range(k): if str1[i] != str2[i]: return False; return True

examples.trace: 1. len('hello')=5, len('help')=4, k=3 → both >=3, continue. 2. i=0: 'h' == 'h' → match. 3. i=1: 'e' == 'e' → match. 4. i=2: 'l' == 'l' → match. 5. All 3 characters match → return True.
1. len('world')=5, len('word')=4, k=4 → both >=4, continue. 2. i=0: 'w' == 'w' → match. 3. i=1: 'o' == 'o' → match. 4. i=2: 'r' == 'r' → match. 5. i=3: 'l' != 'd' → mismatch → return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Accessing characters and substrings in a string
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves getting individual characters or portions of a string. The intuition is that strings are indexed sequences where each character has a position starting from 0. This works because strings are implemented as arrays of characters in most programming languages. Key operations: 1) str[i] gets character at index i. 2) str[start:end] gets substring from index start to end-1. 3) len(str) gets string length. Time complexity for indexing is O(1), for slicing is O(k) where k is slice length. Space complexity for slicing is O(k) for the new substring. This feeds into L3's character comparison, which uses indexing to access individual characters. Common mistakes: index out of bounds errors, misunderstanding slice boundaries (end is exclusive), and negative indexing confusion. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; char = s[0] (gets 'h'); substr = s[0:3] (gets 'hel'); length = len(s) (gets 5)

examples.input: s='programming', get characters at indices 0, 3, 6 and slice 0:4
examples.trace: 1. s[0] = 'p' (first character). 2. s[3] = 'g' (fourth character, indices: 0='p',1='r',2='o',3='g'). 3. s[6] = 'm' (seventh character). 4. s[0:4] = characters from index 0 to 3 inclusive = 'prog'.
1. String is empty, length=0. 2. Trying to access index 0 fails because there are no characters. 3. This would raise an IndexError in most languages.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understanding strings as sequences of characters
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what a string is. A string is a sequence of characters - letters, digits, symbols - treated as a single data unit. The intuition is that text in programming is represented as strings, which are ordered collections where each character has a position. This works because computers represent characters using encoding systems like ASCII or Unicode. Key concepts: 1) Strings are immutable in many languages (can't change individual characters). 2) Strings have length (number of characters). 3) Strings can be empty (''). 4) Characters are the building blocks. Time/space: creating a string is O(n) where n is length, accessing length is O(1). This feeds into L4's indexing operations - you need to understand strings before you can access their parts. Common mistakes: confusing strings with lists, not understanding immutability, and encoding issues. Real code pattern: my_string = 'hello' (creates string), length = len(my_string) (gets 5), empty = '' (empty string)

examples.input: Create string 'cat' and get its length
examples.trace: 1. Define string s = 'cat'. 2. This creates a sequence of 3 characters: 'c', 'a', 't'. 3. Get length using len(s) = 3. 4. The characters are in order: position 0='c', 1='a', 2='t'.

examples.input: Create empty string and check if it equals another empty string
examples.output: Both are empty strings, they are equal
examples.trace: 1. Define s1 = ''. 2. Define s2 = ''. 3. Both strings have length 0 (no characters). 4. Compare s1 == s2 → True because both are empty sequences.

approaches.name: Trie with DFS O(l + k)
approaches.summary: Build a Trie from the dictionary, traverse to the node representing the prefix, then perform DFS to collect all words from that subtree. Suitable for medium-sized datasets with frequent prefix queries.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(l + k) where l is prefix length and k is total characters in suggestions
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(t) where t is total characters in dictionary stored in Trie

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Auto-complete System with Trie and DFS
ladders.desc: Implement a complete auto-complete system that suggests words based on prefix using Trie with DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This full solution implements an auto-complete system where users can add words to a dictionary and query for suggestions based on a prefix. The core approach uses a Trie data structure to efficiently store the dictionary and enable prefix-based lookups. Each Trie node contains children (mapping characters to child nodes), a boolean flag indicating if the node represents the end of a word, and optionally the word itself at terminal nodes. The system has two main operations: insert(word) to add words to the dictionary, and suggest(prefix) to return all words in the dictionary that start with the given prefix. For insertion, we traverse the Trie character by character, creating nodes as needed, and mark the final node as a word end. For suggestions, we first traverse to the node corresponding to the prefix (if it exists), then perform DFS from that node to collect all complete words in its subtree. The DFS recursively explores all child nodes, adding words to the result list when encountering terminal nodes. The time complexity is O(l + k) where l is the prefix length (for traversal to the prefix node) and k is the total characters in all suggestions (for DFS collection). Space complexity is O(t) where t is the total characters stored in the Trie. This approach is optimal for medium-sized datasets with frequent prefix queries because it avoids scanning the entire dictionary for each query. Common mistakes include not handling empty prefix (should return all words), forgetting to mark word ends during insertion, and not properly backtracking during DFS. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the DFS collection subroutine with the Trie structure and prefix traversal.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize empty Trie
2. Insert 'apple': Create nodes for a-p-p-l-e, mark 'e' node as word end with word='apple'
3. Insert 'app': Traverse a-p-p, mark second 'p' node as word end with word='app' (note: this node already exists from 'apple' insertion)
4. Insert 'application': Create nodes for a-p-p-l-i-c-a-t-i-o-n, mark 'n' node as word end with word='application'
5. Insert 'banana': Create nodes for b-a-n-a-n-a, mark last 'a' node as word end
6. Insert 'bat': Create nodes for b-a-t, mark 't' node as word end
7. suggest('app'): Traverse Trie: root→'a'→'p'→'p' (reaches node for prefix 'app')
8. DFS from 'app' node: Current node has word='app' (terminal), add to result
9. DFS explores child 'l': node has no word, continues to child 'e' which has word='apple', add to result
10. DFS backtracks, explores child 'l'→'i'→'c'→'a'→'t'→'i'→'o'→'n' which has word='application', add to result
11. Return sorted result: ['app', 'apple', 'application']

1. Insert all words into Trie
2. suggest('ca'): Traverse to node for 'ca'
3. DFS from 'ca' node: Explore child 'r' (word='car'), add to result
4. From 'r': explore child 'd' (word='card'), add; explore child 't' (word='cart'), add
5. Backtrack, explore child 't' from 'ca' node (word='cat'), add
6. Return ['car', 'card', 'cart', 'cat'] (sorted)
7. suggest('do'): Traverse to node for 'do'
8. DFS: Explore child 'g' (word='dog'), add
9. Return ['dog']

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Collection from Trie Node
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to collect all complete words from a given Trie node's subtree.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the suggestion mechanism. Given a Trie node (which represents some prefix), we need to collect all complete words that exist in the subtree rooted at that node. The intuition is that once we reach the node corresponding to the user's prefix input, all words with that prefix must be in the subtree below that node. We perform depth-first search (DFS) to systematically explore all possible paths from the current node. At each node, we check if it represents the end of a word (has is_end=True). If so, we add the associated word to our results list. Then we recursively explore all child nodes (each representing a possible next character). The DFS ensures we visit every node in the subtree exactly once. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the total number of characters in all words in the subtree (since we visit each node once). Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack where h is the height of the subtree. This connects to L0 as the critical component that generates suggestions after prefix traversal. Common mistakes include not handling the base case properly (empty node), forgetting to add the word when is_end=True, and not exploring all children. The DFS must be exhaustive to ensure all matching words are found.

examples.input:
Trie with words: ['cat', 'car', 'card']
Start node: node for prefix 'ca'
examples.trace:
1. Start at node for 'ca'
2. Check: node.is_end = False (no word here), continue
3. Explore children: 'r' and 't'
4. Go to child 'r': node.is_end = True (word='car'), add 'car' to result
5. From 'r' node, explore child 'd': node.is_end = True (word='card'), add 'card'
6. Backtrack to 'ca' node
7. Go to child 't': node.is_end = True (word='cat'), add 'cat'
8. Return result ['car', 'card', 'cat']

examples.input:
Trie with words: ['a', 'ab', 'abc']
Start node: root node
examples.trace:
1. Start at root
2. Explore child 'a': node.is_end = True (word='a'), add 'a'
3. From 'a' node, explore child 'b': node.is_end = True (word='ab'), add 'ab'
4. From 'b' node, explore child 'c': node.is_end = True (word='abc'), add 'abc'
5. Return ['a', 'ab', 'abc']

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trie Prefix Traversal and Subtree Identification
ladders.desc: Traverse the Trie to find the node representing a given prefix, enabling subtree access for suggestions.
ladders.explanation: This core logic enables the prefix-based lookup that makes auto-complete efficient. The intuition is that a Trie organizes words by their prefixes, so all words sharing a common prefix will be in the same subtree. Given a prefix string, we start at the root of the Trie and follow the path corresponding to each character in the prefix. For each character, we check if the current node has a child for that character. If yes, we move to that child node. If at any point the required child doesn't exist, it means no words in the dictionary have that prefix, so we return null/empty. If we successfully traverse all characters in the prefix, we arrive at the node representing that exact prefix. This node serves as the root of the subtree containing all words with that prefix. The time complexity is O(l) where l is the prefix length, as we perform one operation per character. Space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the Trie itself. This connects to L1 because once we have this node, we can pass it to the DFS collection routine. Common mistakes include not checking for null children at each step, not handling empty prefix correctly (should return root node), and not distinguishing between 'prefix exists but no words' vs 'prefix doesn't exist'. The traversal must be exact character-by-character to ensure we reach the correct subtree.

examples.trace:
1. Start at root
2. Character 'c': root has child 'c', move to 'c' node
3. Character 'a': 'c' node has child 'a', move to 'ca' node
4. Character 'r': 'ca' node has child 'r', move to 'car' node
5. All characters processed, return 'car' node

1. Start at root
2. 'h': root has child 'h', move to 'h' node
3. 'e': 'h' node has child 'e', move to 'he' node
4. 'a': 'he' node has no child 'a', traversal fails
5. Return null

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Node Structure and Word Insertion
ladders.desc: Define the Trie node structure and implement insertion of words into the Trie.
ladders.explanation: This building block establishes the fundamental data structure that enables efficient prefix searches. A Trie (prefix tree) node needs to store: 1) children mapping (character → child node), 2) a boolean flag indicating if this node represents the end of a valid word, and 3) optionally the complete word string at terminal nodes for easy retrieval. The intuition is that words sharing common prefixes will share nodes in the Trie, saving space and enabling efficient prefix-based operations. For insertion, we start at the root and process each character of the word sequentially. For each character, we check if the current node has a child for that character. If not, we create a new node and add it as a child. We then move to that child node. After processing all characters, we mark the final node as a word end (is_end=True) and store the word there if desired. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the word length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(m) in the worst case if no prefixes are shared (though typically less due to sharing). This connects to L2 because we need a populated Trie before we can traverse it for prefixes. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark the final node as a word end, not creating new nodes when needed, and not properly initializing child mappings. The insertion must be exact to ensure words can be found later during prefix searches.

examples.trace:
1. Start at root (empty)
2. Character 'c': root has no child 'c', create new node for 'c', add as child of root
3. Move to 'c' node
4. Character 'a': 'c' node has no child 'a', create new node for 'a', add as child
5. Move to 'ca' node
6. Character 't': 'ca' node has no child 't', create new node for 't', add as child
7. Move to 'cat' node
8. Mark 'cat' node as is_end=True, store word='cat'
9. Trie now has path: root→'c'→'a'→'t' with 't' node marked as word end

1. Start at root
2. 'c': root has child 'c', move to 'c' node
3. 'a': 'c' node has child 'a', move to 'ca' node
4. 'r': 'ca' node has no child 'r', create new node for 'r', add as child
5. Move to 'car' node
6. Mark 'car' node as is_end=True, store word='car'
7. Trie now shares 'c'→'a' path for both words, then branches to 't' (cat) and 'r' (car)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character-by-Character String Traversal with Node Navigation
ladders.desc: Process a string character by character, navigating through connected nodes.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to both Trie insertion and prefix search. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we need to process them sequentially while maintaining a current position in a node-based structure. We start with a current node (typically the root). For each character in the input string, we check if the current node has a child node corresponding to that character. If it does, we update the current node to that child node. If it doesn't, we have two options depending on the context: during insertion, we create a new child node; during search, we terminate with failure. After processing all characters, the final current node represents the end of that string path in the structure. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the string length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(1) additional space. This connects to L3 as the core mechanism for building and querying the Trie. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop indices, not handling empty strings properly, and not updating the current node correctly. The operation must be precise to ensure we follow the exact path corresponding to the input string.

examples.input:
String: 'hello'
Start node: root with children 'h'→'e'→'l'→'l'→'o'
examples.trace:
1. Current node = root
2. Character 'h': root has child 'h', current = 'h' node
3. Character 'e': 'h' node has child 'e', current = 'he' node
4. Character 'l': 'he' node has child 'l', current = 'hel' node
5. Character 'l': 'hel' node has child 'l', current = 'hell' node
6. Character 'o': 'hell' node has child 'o', current = 'hello' node
7. Return 'hello' node

examples.input:
String: 'world'
Start node: root with only 'h' child
examples.trace:
1. Current node = root
2. Character 'w': root has no child 'w', traversal fails
3. Return null

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Tree Node Structure with Children Mapping
ladders.desc: Create a basic tree node that can store multiple child references using a dictionary.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept establishes the basic building block for the Trie data structure. A tree node is a simple object that contains data and references to its child nodes. In our case, we need a node that can have multiple children (unlike a binary tree which has exactly two). We use a dictionary to map characters to child nodes because it provides O(1) lookup time for checking if a particular child exists. Each node needs two main attributes: 1) children: a dictionary where keys are characters and values are child node objects, and 2) is_end: a boolean flag that indicates whether this node represents the end of a valid word. The intuition is that this structure allows us to build paths where each node represents a prefix, and multiple words can share common prefix paths. Time complexity for checking if a child exists is O(1) due to dictionary lookup. Space complexity per node is O(c) where c is the number of distinct children. This connects to L4 as the fundamental structure we navigate during character-by-character traversal. Common mistakes include not initializing the children dictionary properly, confusing node references, and not setting default values for attributes. Understanding this simple node structure is crucial before building more complex Trie operations.

examples.input: Create a node with children 'a' and 'b'
examples.output: Node with children dict {'a': node_a, 'b': node_b}
examples.trace:
1. Create parent node: children = {}, is_end = False
2. Create child node for 'a': children_a = {}, is_end = False
3. Create child node for 'b': children_b = {}, is_end = False
4. Add to parent: parent.children['a'] = node_a
5. Add to parent: parent.children['b'] = node_b
6. Parent node now has children dict with 2 entries

1. Given node with children {'a': node_a, 'b': node_b}
2. Check 'c' in node.children
3. 'c' is not a key in the dictionary
4. Return False

approaches.name: Trie with BFS and Caching O(l + s)
approaches.summary: Construct a Trie and use BFS from the prefix node to retrieve suggestions level by level, optionally caching frequent queries. Ideal for systems requiring real-time responses and handling repeated prefixes.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(l + s) where l is prefix length and s is number of suggestions
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(t + c) where t is Trie storage and c is cache size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Auto-complete System with Trie, BFS, and Caching
ladders.desc: Implement a complete auto-complete system that suggests words based on prefix using Trie with BFS traversal and caching for repeated queries.
ladders.explanation: This solution builds a Trie data structure to store words, then for each query prefix, it navigates to the corresponding node in the Trie and performs BFS to collect suggestions level by level. A cache stores recent queries to optimize repeated requests. The intuition is that Trie provides O(l) prefix lookup, BFS ensures we get suggestions in breadth-first order (which can be useful for alphabetical or frequency-based ordering), and caching reduces repeated computation for common prefixes. Step-by-step: 1. Build Trie with nodes containing children map, is_end flag, and optionally frequency. 2. For insert(word): traverse Trie, creating nodes as needed, mark end node. 3. For get_suggestions(prefix): find prefix node by traversing characters; if not found, return empty list. 4. From prefix node, perform BFS: use queue initialized with prefix node; while queue not empty, pop node, if node.is_end, add word (reconstructed via path tracking) to suggestions; enqueue all children. Limit suggestions to top k (e.g., 3). 5. Cache: maintain dictionary mapping prefix to suggestions; update on each query if not present. Time: O(l + s) where l is prefix length for traversal, s is number of nodes visited in BFS (up to total nodes in worst case). Space: O(t + c) for Trie storage and cache. Connection to L1: This integrates the BFS suggestion retrieval from L1 with Trie operations and caching. Common mistakes: not handling empty prefix, forgetting to reconstruct words during BFS, incorrect cache invalidation on new inserts. Real code pattern: class TrieNode with children dict and is_end; class AutocompleteSystem with insert, search_prefix, bfs_suggestions methods.

examples.trace: 1. Build Trie: root -> 'c' -> 'a' -> 't' (is_end=True for 'cat'), 'r' (is_end=True for 'car') -> 't' (is_end=True for 'cart'); 'd' -> 'o' -> 'g' (is_end=True for 'dog'). 2. Query 'ca': traverse from root: 'c' -> 'a', found prefix node at 'a'. 3. BFS from 'a': queue = [node_a]; pop node_a, not end (prefix incomplete). Enqueue children: node_t, node_r. Queue = [node_t, node_r]; pop node_t, is_end=True, reconstruct word: 'cat', add to suggestions. Enqueue children: none. Queue = [node_r]; pop node_r, is_end=True, reconstruct: 'car', add. Enqueue children: node_t. Queue = [node_t]; pop node_t, is_end=True, reconstruct: 'cart', add. Suggestions = ['cat', 'car', 'cart']. 4. Cache: store 'ca': ['cat', 'car', 'cart'].

1. Build Trie: root -> 'h' -> 'e' -> 'l' -> 'l' (is_end=True for 'hell') -> 'o' (is_end=True for 'hello'); 'p' (is_end=True for 'help'); 'a' -> 'p' (is_end=True for 'heap'). 2. Query 'he': traverse: 'h' -> 'e', prefix node at 'e'. 3. BFS from 'e': queue = [node_e]; pop node_e, not end. Enqueue children: node_l, node_p, node_a. Queue = [node_l, node_p, node_a]; pop node_l, not end. Enqueue children: node_l (second 'l'). Queue = [node_p, node_a, node_l]; pop node_p, is_end=True, reconstruct: 'help', add. Queue = [node_a, node_l]; pop node_a, not end. Enqueue children: node_p. Queue = [node_l, node_p]; pop node_l, is_end=True, reconstruct: 'hell', add. Queue = [node_p]; pop node_p, is_end=True, reconstruct: 'heap', add but limit to top 3, so ignore. Suggestions = ['help', 'hell', 'hello'] (order from BFS, but typically sort; here assume BFS order yields 'hello' after 'hell' due to traversal). Actually, BFS order: after 'help', 'hell', then 'hello' from deeper node. So output ['hello', 'help', 'hell'] if we collect all and sort alphabetically. For simplicity, we take first 3 in BFS order: 'help', 'hell', 'hello' -> sort to ['hello', 'help', 'hell'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Suggestion Retrieval from Trie Node
ladders.desc: Given a Trie node representing a prefix, perform BFS to collect all complete words in its subtree.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of generating suggestions once the prefix node is found. The intuition is that BFS traverses the Trie level by level from the prefix node, allowing us to collect words in a breadth-first order, which can be useful for ranking (e.g., by length or alphabetically). Step-by-step: 1. Input: a Trie node (prefix_node) and optionally a limit k for number of suggestions. 2. Initialize an empty list for suggestions and a queue with (prefix_node, current_word) where current_word is the prefix string. 3. While queue is not empty and suggestions size < k: a. Pop node and word from queue. b. If node.is_end is True, add word to suggestions. c. For each child in node.children: enqueue (child, word + child_char). 4. Return suggestions. Time: O(s) where s is the number of nodes visited in BFS (up to total nodes in worst case). Space: O(s) for queue storage. Connection to L0: This function is called after finding the prefix node in the full problem. Common mistakes: not reconstructing words correctly during BFS, infinite loops if cycles exist (but Trie is acyclic), not limiting suggestions. Real code pattern: def bfs_suggestions(node, prefix): queue = [(node, prefix)]; suggestions = []; while queue: curr, word = queue.pop(0); if curr.is_end: suggestions.append(word); for char, child in curr.children.items(): queue.append((child, word + char)); return suggestions.

examples.input: Trie with words: ['cat', 'car', 'cart']; Prefix node at 'a' (after 'ca'); Limit: 3
examples.trace: 1. Start at node_a with word='ca'. Queue = [(node_a, 'ca')]. 2. Pop (node_a, 'ca'): node_a.is_end=False. Enqueue children: node_t with word='cat', node_r with word='car'. Queue = [(node_t, 'cat'), (node_r, 'car')]. 3. Pop (node_t, 'cat'): node_t.is_end=True, add 'cat' to suggestions. Suggestions = ['cat']. Enqueue children: none. Queue = [(node_r, 'car')]. 4. Pop (node_r, 'car'): node_r.is_end=True, add 'car'. Suggestions = ['cat', 'car']. Enqueue children: node_t with word='cart'. Queue = [(node_t, 'cart')]. 5. Pop (node_t, 'cart'): node_t.is_end=True, add 'cart'. Suggestions = ['cat', 'car', 'cart']. Limit 3 reached, return.

examples.input: Trie with words: ['he', 'hello', 'help']; Prefix node at 'e' (after 'he'); Limit: 2
examples.trace: 1. Start at node_e with word='he'. Queue = [(node_e, 'he')]. 2. Pop (node_e, 'he'): node_e.is_end=True? Assume 'he' is a word, so is_end=True, add 'he'. Suggestions = ['he']. Enqueue children: node_l, node_p. Queue = [(node_l, 'hel'), (node_p, 'hep')]. 3. Pop (node_l, 'hel'): node_l.is_end=False. Enqueue children: node_l (second 'l') with word='hell'. Queue = [(node_p, 'hep'), (node_l, 'hell')]. 4. Pop (node_p, 'hep'): node_p.is_end=False. Enqueue children: none. Queue = [(node_l, 'hell')]. 5. Pop (node_l, 'hell'): node_l.is_end=False. Enqueue children: node_o with word='hello'. Queue = [(node_o, 'hello')]. 6. Pop (node_o, 'hello'): node_o.is_end=True, add 'hello'. Suggestions = ['he', 'hello']. Limit 2 reached, return. Note: 'help' not included due to limit.

testCases.input: Node with is_end=True, no children; Prefix word: 'a'; Limit: 5
Node with multiple levels: 'a'->'b'->end, 'a'->'c'->end; Prefix: 'start'; Limit: 2
Node with no end in subtree; Prefix: 'test'; Limit: 3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trie Prefix Search and Node Navigation
ladders.desc: Given a prefix string, traverse the Trie from the root to find the node corresponding to the last character of the prefix.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for efficient prefix lookup in a Trie. The intuition is that a Trie stores characters along paths, so by following the characters of the prefix from the root, we can reach the node representing that prefix in O(l) time, where l is prefix length. If any character is missing, the prefix doesn't exist in the Trie. Step-by-step: 1. Start at the root node. 2. For each character in the prefix: a. Check if the current node has a child with that character. b. If yes, move to that child node. c. If no, return None (prefix not found). 3. After processing all characters, return the current node (which represents the prefix). Time: O(l) for traversing l characters. Space: O(1) extra space. Connection to L1: This node is then passed to the BFS routine to get suggestions. Common mistakes: not handling empty prefix (should return root), assuming the prefix node itself is a word (it may not have is_end=True). Real code pattern: def search_prefix(prefix): node = root; for char in prefix: if char not in node.children: return None; node = node.children[char]; return node.

examples.trace: 1. Start at root. 2. First char 'c': root has child 'c', move to node_c. 3. Second char 'a': node_c has child 'a', move to node_a. 4. Prefix 'ca' processed, return node_a.
1. Start at root. 2. 'h': root has child 'h', move to node_h. 3. 'e': node_h has child 'e', move to node_e. 4. 'l': node_e has child 'l', move to node_l. 5. Return node_l.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Insertion Operation
ladders.desc: Insert a word into a Trie data structure by creating nodes as needed and marking the end node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for building the Trie that stores the dictionary of words. The intuition is that each character of the word corresponds to a node in the Trie, and we traverse from the root, creating new nodes for characters not already present, then mark the final node as the end of a word. This ensures efficient prefix sharing among words. Step-by-step: 1. Start at the root node. 2. For each character in the word: a. Check if the current node has a child with that character. b. If not, create a new TrieNode and add it as a child. c. Move to the child node. 3. After processing all characters, set the current node's is_end flag to True. Time: O(l) where l is word length. Space: O(l) in worst case if all characters are new nodes. Connection to L2: Insertion populates the Trie so that prefix search can work. Common mistakes: forgetting to mark the end node, not handling duplicate inserts (should be idempotent), incorrect node creation. Real code pattern: def insert(word): node = root; for char in word: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[char]; node.is_end = True.

examples.trace: 1. Start at root. 2. Char 'c': not in root.children, create node_c, add to root. Move to node_c. 3. Char 'a': not in node_c.children, create node_a, add to node_c. Move to node_a. 4. Char 't': not in node_a.children, create node_t, add to node_a. Move to node_t. 5. Set node_t.is_end = True.

1. Start at root. 2. 'c': root has child 'c', move to node_c. 3. 'a': node_c has child 'a', move to node_a. 4. 'r': node_a does not have child 'r', create node_r, add to node_a. Move to node_r. 5. Set node_r.is_end = True. Now Trie: root->'c'->'a'->'t' (end) and 'r' (end).

testCases.expected: Root->'d'->'o'->'g' (end), no change on second insert

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Trie Node Structure and Child Management
ladders.desc: Define a Trie node with children mapping and an end flag, and perform basic operations like adding a child or checking for a child.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves the basic building block of a Trie: the node. Each node represents a character in the Trie and contains a mapping to its child nodes (typically a dictionary) and a boolean flag to indicate if it marks the end of a word. The intuition is that this structure allows efficient traversal and storage of prefixes. Step-by-step: 1. Define a TrieNode class with attributes: children (dict) and is_end (bool). 2. To add a child: given a character, create a new TrieNode and add it to the children dictionary with the character as key. 3. To check for a child: look up the character in the children dictionary. Time: O(1) for add/check operations. Space: O(1) per node, but overall depends on number of nodes. Connection to L3: This node structure is used in insertion to create and link nodes. Common mistakes: using a list instead of dict for children (less efficient), not initializing is_end to False. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. Then, node.children[char] = TrieNode() to add; char in node.children to check.

examples.trace: 1. Initialize root: children={}, is_end=False. 2. Add child 'a': create new TrieNode, set root.children['a'] = new node. Now root.children = {'a': node_a}.

examples.input: Node with children 'b' and 'c', check for 'b' and 'd'
examples.trace: 1. Node has children = {'b': node_b, 'c': node_c}. 2. Check 'b': 'b' in children -> True. 3. Check 'd': 'd' in children -> False.

testCases.expected: Depends on node_a, but typically False unless set

testCases.input: Node: children={'cat': node} (invalid, should be char)
testCases.expected: Not standard, but assume key is char

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Tree Node Structure with Children Mapping
ladders.desc: Create a basic tree node that can store multiple child references using a dictionary.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for working with Tries, as Trie operations involve processing strings character by character. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and by looping through them, we can build or search structures like Tries. Step-by-step: 1. Given a string, use a loop (e.g., for or while) to access each character from first to last. 2. For each character, perform an operation such as checking equality, adding to a new string, or using it as a key. Time: O(n) where n is string length. Space: O(1) if no extra storage. Connection to L4: This traversal is used to add children to Trie nodes or check for characters. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors, not handling empty strings, case sensitivity issues. Real code pattern: for char in string: # do something with char. Example: build new string: result = ''; for char in string: result += char.

examples.trace: 1. Initialize: string = 'hello'. 2. Loop: char = 'h', print 'h'. 3. char = 'e', print 'e'. 4. char = 'l', print 'l'. 5. char = 'l', print 'l'. 6. char = 'o', print 'o'.

examples.input: String: 'cat'; Build new string by adding '!' after each char
examples.trace: 1. Start with result = ''. 2. char = 'c', result = 'c!'. 3. char = 'a', result = 'c!a!'. 4. char = 't', result = 'c!a!t!'.

testCases.input: String: '123'; Concatenate each char to form '123'
